name: Tiny Grimes
abstract: Lloyd "Tiny" Grimes (July 7, 1916 – March 4, 1989) was an American jazz and R&B guitarist. He was a member of the Art Tatum Trio from 1943 to 1944, was a backing musician on recording sessions, and later led his own bands, including a recording session with Charlie Parker.

concerts.label: New York Jazz Repertory Company: 52nd Street Revisited
New York Jazz Repertory Company: Seventy Five Years of Jazz

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: Billy Taylor on bass, Billy Taylor who used to work with Duke Ellington, myself on piano, Tiny Grimes on guitar, Don Bias on tenor, and Cozy Cole on drums. We played a Broadway show... called the Seven Lively Arts.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: James Chirillo
about_quotes.evidence: What was it like studying with Tiny Grimes? ... I really got a concept of sound from him too. ... I first met him and started studying with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Chirillo interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: Later, who’d I work with? Tiny Grimes... It was Tiny Grimes and his Swinging Highlanders. So I had to wear the kilts.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Chuck Green
about_quotes.evidence: And we happened to get these virtuso musicians, you know, like jazz -- Tiny Grimes, Professor Trevorson, you see, and Jerry Citron, and Pete Peterson, you know. And we already had about three others. Well, it was a combo, you know.

about_quotes.speaker: Alvin Queen
about_quotes.evidence: And I spent many, many years as you know, with Horace Silver and George Benson and Wild Bill Davis and Tiny Grimes... And then Tiny Grimes, that’s when I worked with Tiny Grimes at that time
about_quotes.doc_title: Alvin Queen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 23, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: David Albert Francis
about_quotes.evidence: my first trip over there was with Milt Buckner, and Arnett Cobb, Tiny Grimes, and a bass player from France named Roland Lavesoir
